request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"rethinkdb::devtools","name":"rethinkdb","one_liner":"Free, open-source distributed document-oriented database with real-time change-feed support, built for developers, that failed to convert its OSS adoption into a sustainable commercial revenue stream.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"RethinkDB was free/open-source with no meaningful monetization (price_point: free, monetization: other), whereas the new pitch is a B2B SaaS with a hosted control plane — implying recurring subscription or usage-based revenue. Both target developers, but the commercial model is fundamentally different: the new pitch starts commercial-first."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both serve the developer/infrastructure tooling market (customer_type: developer, geography: global) and operate in the cloud-native ecosystem. However, RethinkDB targeted the database layer, while the new pitch targets ephemeral preview environments — an adjacent but distinct segment of the developer platform market."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both rely on a developer-led, bottom-up GTM: CLI tooling, open community, and GitHub-centric distribution. RethinkDB grew through OSS adoption, community drivers, and YC credibility. The new pitch uses a CLI plus a hosted control plane — the CLI suggests a similar grassroots developer-pull motion, though the hosted control plane implies a faster path to monetization."},"stage_scale":{"score":6,"rationale":"RethinkDB operated for ~7 years (2009-2016) before shutdown and reached a point of broad community awareness but never scaled revenue. The new pitch appears to be at an early stage. Both share the challenge of being early-stage infrastructure plays with long adoption cycles and high churn risk before commercial traction solidifies."}},"why_similar":"Both RethinkDB and the new pitch are developer-first infrastructure products distributed partly via CLI tooling, targeting a global developer audience in the cloud-native ecosystem. Both face the canonical open-source/developer-tooling dilemma: building a large technical community that loves the product without generating proportional commercial revenue. RethinkDB's trajectory — YC-backed, technically lauded, community-grown, then unable to build a sustainable business — is a direct archetype for any developer-infrastructure startup that prioritizes adoption over monetization.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization model: The new pitch is explicitly B2B SaaS with a hosted control plane (implying subscription or usage-based revenue), whereas RethinkDB was free OSS with no viable commercial tier — this is the single most important divergence. 2. Product category: Preview-environment orchestration across cloud providers vs. a distributed database — adjacent infrastructure but a different buyer problem. 3. Competitive moat: RethinkDB competed head-on with MongoDB and other document databases in a commoditizing market; ephemeral preview environments are a narrower, less commoditized niche at time of writing. 4. Open-source strategy: RethinkDB's entire product was open-sourced; the new pitch has a commercial hosted control plane that is presumably proprietary, giving a clearer path to capturing value.","failure_causes":["inability to monetize OSS adoption","no viable commercial tier or enterprise offering","market timing mismatch — real-time DB features arrived before broad demand","intense competition from MongoDB and other document databases","community adoption did not convert to paying customers","loss of engineering team to acqui-hire (Stripe), signaling internal collapse","free price point eliminated revenue leverage"],"lessons_for_input":["Launch your paid hosted control plane before — not after — achieving community scale; do not let free usage become the default expectation.","Define a concrete upgrade trigger (e.g., team seats, environment concurrency, SSO) that converts CLI users into paying control-plane customers from day one.","Validate willingness-to-pay with at least 10 paying teams before investing heavily in open-source community growth.","Avoid competing on a dimension (e.g., raw feature parity with incumbents) that commoditizes your product; anchor pricing and positioning on the operational pain you uniquely solve.","Retain your engineering team with equity and milestones tied to revenue, not just adoption metrics, so a talent acqui-hire does not become the exit path."]}
